ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vietnamese Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi; mapped to closest chicken rice dish concept. FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Vietnamese garlic fish sauce chicken rice.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat dish — rice-based composite dish with sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi. Mapped to composite chicken-rice dish category A0FJP as the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi typically weighs approximately 550 g and includes a portion of steamed jasmine rice, pan-fried or stir-fried chicken pieces coated in garlic fish sauce, and fresh accompaniments such as cucumber slices, tomato, and coriander.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken (pan-fried), cooked white rice, and fish sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-component modelling using FAO/INFOODS SEA values for cooked jasmine rice (~55% by weight), USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for pan-fried chicken thigh (~35% by weight), and fish sauce/garlic/vegetable garnish (~10% by weight). Input per_100g values (energy 136.4 kcal, protein 8.2 g, fat 5.5 g, carbohydrate 13.6 g) are consistent with this modelled composition and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 327 mg/100g reflects the fish sauce contribution and is plausible. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available from the component sources at this level of disaggregation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, jasmine)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi as a composite dish. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being boiled jasmine white rice (GI ~68–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; mean 68 used). The chicken and fish sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 75.0) / 100 = 51.0, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 13.64 × 5.5 = 75.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce (mắm tỏi dressing): 327 mg/100g, equivalent to approximately 1,800 mg sodium per standard 550 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request the garlic fish sauce dressing on the side or ask for a reduced-sodium preparation, as a full serving provides approximately 1,800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — primarily from fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~51 per serving) driven by white jasmine rice (GI ~68) with low dietary fibre (0.73 g/100g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size of this dish or substitute white jasmine rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, as a standard 550 g serving delivers approximately 75 g of carbohydrate with very low fibre, resulting in rapid postprandial glycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.45 g/100g; ~8 g per serving) and elevated sodium (327 mg/100g; ~1,800 mg per serving) from fish sauce, with low trans fat (0.04 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content from fish sauce is the primary concern and warrants portion control or sauce reduction; saturated fat is moderate and within acceptable limits for a single meal, but cumulative daily intake should be monitored.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Presence of nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) which may trigger joint inflammation in nightshade-sensitive RA individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should request omission of tomato and chilli from the dish, and ask for reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi (Vietnamese Chicken Rice with Garlic Fish Sauce) is a popular Vietnamese rice dish consisting of steamed fragrant jasmine rice served alongside pan-fried or stir-fried chicken pieces — typically thigh or breast — that are coated in a bold, savoury-sweet garlic fish sauce dressing (mắm tỏi). The sauce is made from fish sauce (nước mắm), minced garlic, chilli, sugar, and lime juice, producing a pungent, umami-rich glaze with a balance of salty, sweet, sour, and spicy notes. The dish is typically garnished with fresh cucumber slices, tomato wedges, and coriander, providing textural contrast and freshness. The chicken has a lightly caramelised exterior from the stir-frying process and a tender, juicy interior. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 5.5 g fat, and 13.6 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 327 mg/100g reflecting the fish sauce component. The glycemic index is estimated at 68 (medium) based on the dominant white jasmine rice component, and a standard 550 g plate delivers a glycemic load of approximately 51. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium (~1,800 mg per serving) and for type 2 diabetes due to the high glycemic load from white rice with minimal fibre (0.73 g/100g). It is moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course in Vietnam and Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia and Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Cơm Gà Sốt Mắm Tỏi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component modelling using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the constituent components (jasmine rice, pan-fried chicken, fish sauce). The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this modelled composition and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant white jasmine rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid disaggregation for this composite Vietnamese chicken rice dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in the approved sources consulted (FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) as of 2026. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on boiled jasmine white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation with chicken and fish sauce; individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 5. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, but the dish is of Vietnamese culinary origin; region_of_origin has been corrected to Vietnam. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100g is retained from the input; this low value is plausible for a pan-fried chicken preparation and is not confirmed zero — it reflects a trace amount from the cooking fat.
